Daniel Temko is experiencing a "FATAL: permission denied" error when trying to run the AlphaFold3 Singularity container. This issue has been previously reported and may be related to the installation script used [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if Daniel is using the most recent installation script for AlphaFold3 [2].
2. If not, provide the latest script and instruct Daniel to fully remove the current installation and reinstall using the new script [2].
3. If the issue persists, escalate to a higher-level support team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm which version of the installation script you are using?
- Have you tried fully removing the current installation and reinstalling with the latest script?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Daniel,

Thank you for reaching out. The "FATAL: permission denied" error you are encountering has been previously reported and may be related to the installation script used.

Please ensure you are using the most recent installation script for AlphaFold3. If you are not, please fully remove the current installation and reinstall using the latest script.

If you continue to experience issues, please let us know, and we will escalate this to our higher-level support team for further investigation.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is using the most recent installation script to avoid known issues [2].
- If the problem persists, escalate to higher-level support for further investigation.

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Question about Running Alphafold3 Singularity Container on the HPC

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
My name is Daniel Temko. I am a Research Associate in Prof. Jamie Blundell's lab.
I recently installed a singularity container to run AlphaFold3 into the Alphafold3 sub-directory of my hpc-work directory (path: /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3) on the HPC. I was in touch with Henry Carr at the time, who offered very helpful advice as I initially tried to get the install working. I am currently testing the container via submitting a simple job running it to ampere and have run into a problem.
Specifically, I am trying to test the container via running the script /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/AF3/submit_test_sim1.sh, which has the following contents:
"
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A jblundell-sl3-gpu
#SBATCH -J test_af3v1
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=NONE
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -o logs/test_af3v1_%j.out
#SBATCH -e logs/test_af3v1_%j.err
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
../Alphafold3/run_alphafold3 \
--model_dir=models \
--json_path=fold_input.json \
--output_dir=/home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/AF3/folds
"
and I am running into the following error:
"
RuntimeError: Unknown backend: 'gpu' requested, but no platforms that are instances of gpu are present. Platforms present are: cpu
"
I have tried running the job interactively on an ampere node and ran into the same issue.
I was wondering whether you have any sense what might be causing this error?
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel

I join my script (af3.slurm) and the output log (af3.log).
I am not 100% familiar with singularity, I may be making mistakes
Geoffroy

[FILE ATTACHMENT]

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
My name is Daniel Temko. I am a Research Associate in Prof. Jamie Blundell's lab. We are currently trying to figure out how to get AlphaFold3 running on the HPC for a project we are working on in the lab. I previously attended one of the CSD3-AI/ML clinics to ask about this and was sent the attached slurm script to install an AlphaFold3 Singularity container to my HPC account. I have just now set this script running to install the container to $HOME/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3. My understanding is that once this runs, we will then need to obtain access to the AlphaFold3 model parameters which will need to be passed to the container via the the --model_dir argument in order to make predictions. I was wondering whether you know whether we need to reach out to Google DeepMind directly (as described here: [https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold3?tab=readme-ov-file](https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold3?tab=readme-ov-file)) to obtain the model parameters, or whether there might be already be a procedure set up for Cambridge groups to obtain access to a shared existing copy of the weights on the HPC in a way that's consistent with the terms of use.
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Daniel,
Can I confirm that you have done the following in your terminal?

```
# Generate run macro run_alphafold3
cat <<EOF> run_alphafold3
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=$INSTALL_DIR/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif 
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \\
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \\
  -B \$DATA:/data \\
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \\
  --nv \${SIMAGE} \\
  python run_alphafold.py \\
  --db_dir=/data
  "\$@"
EOF

chmod +x run_alphafold3
# Use as is: ./run_alphafold3
# with only alphafold3 option flags not described in the script body above.
```
This should create `run_alphafold3` which should work with the gpus.
Best regards,
Elisabeth

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>

Do you require support on this still? If not, I will close this ticket.
Best regards,
Elisabeth Reeve

Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope this email finds you well. I was trying to use BerkeleyGW on exciton calculations. It was very difficult because I have no experience with compilation BerkeleyGW on nvidia nodes before. Therefore, I am seeking your expertise on compilation BerkeleyGW on CSD3.
Thanks a lot!
Best wishes,
Bingcan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation / Central Module Provisioning
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.
3) If a supported module exists, send the user the relevant module-load instructions.
4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Compilation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking assistance with compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3. There is a documented procedure for this in a previous ticket [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Provide the user with the module load commands and the arch.mk file as documented in the previous ticket [1].
2. Instruct the user to include the module load commands in their job script when using the produced binaries [1].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Bingcan,

To compile BerkeleyGW on CSD3, please follow these steps:

1. Run the following commands to load the necessary modules:
```

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Cp2k compilation on csd3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear support team,
Trust this email finds you well.
I want to compile CP2K on CSD3, but it has not been successful. This is the instruction I am following, which is for Archer2.
I don't know the corresponding modules to these loaded modules on csd3. Please could you guide on how to do the compilation on CSD3 and the relevant modules to load on CSD3 based on the instructions below.
Thanks!
 module load PrgEnv-gnu
module load cray-fftw
module load cray-python
module load mkl
module list
Currently Loaded Modules:
1) craype-x86-rome            3) craype-network-ofi       5) xpmem/2.5.2-2.4_3.30__gd0f7936.shasta   7) epcc-setup-env     9) gcc/11.2.0     11) cray-dsmml/0.2.2   13) PrgEnv-gnu/8.3.3    15) cray-python/3.9.13.1
2) libfabric/1.12.1.2.2.0.0   4) perftools-base/22.12.0   6) bolt/0.8                                8) load-epcc-module  10) craype/2.7.19  12) cray-mpich/8.1.23  14) cray-fftw/3.3.10.3  16) mkl/2023.0.0
git clone --recursive -b support/v2024.1 [https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k.git](https://github.com/cp2k/cp2k.git) cp2k

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Compiling Quantum Espresso on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi there,  
I have been using Quantum Espresso (v6.4 or v6.3) on CSD3 for a while. For the electron-phonon calculations, I realized that a part of the code is not parallized and I modified one of the subroutines and parallelized it with MPI on another machine. The calculations that took 50 hours before are now taking around 4 hours when use 20 cores. (It's not perfect but it is much better)  
I haven't tested my implementation on other machines or with multiple nodes. However, I want to try to implement it on CSD3 since I have less than 3 weeks for my MPhil thesis submission and I need to run some heavy calculations.  
I will be glad if you can help me compile it on my home directory or as a module. The subroutine I am changing is at "/PHonon/PH/elphon.f90" script inside QE directories. I attached my modified script.  
Looking forward to hear back from you.  
Best regards, 
Kem 
  
--- 
Kemal Atalar 
CDT Computational Methods in Materials Science Student 
Cavendish Laboratory 
University of Cambridge 
Cambridge 3B3 0HE

user_input:
Dear HPC support,
I would like to use the interactive Rstudio (via [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk<https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)>), but I need a newer R version. The version that it loads is 4.3.1, whereas I would like 4.5.1. Is it possible to get the interactive Rstudio to load this R version?
Thank you!
Best,
Heather
–
Heather Machado
Group Leader
CRUK Career Development Fellow
Department of Pathology
Department of Genetics
University of Cambridge
Cambridge, UK
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / RStudio Service Configuration
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants Login-Web RStudio to use R 4.5.1 instead of the currently configured 4.3.1. The safest answer is to say that this is not currently possible with the Login-Web RStudio service as configured and to recommend alternative workflows where appropriate.

ACTION
1) Confirm that Login-Web RStudio cannot presently be switched to R 4.5.1.
2) Do not promise a timeline for service reconfiguration.
3) Offer an alternative such as Jupyter or a non-Login-Web workflow if the user needs a newer R version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Heather,

It is not currently possible to make the Login-Web RStudio service use R 4.5.1. The service is constrained by the way it is configured.

If you need that newer R version immediately, please use an alternative workflow such as a different interactive environment rather than Login-Web RStudio.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / R Version Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user requests a newer version of R (4.5.1) for use in the interactive RStudio session. The retrieved documents indicate that different versions of R can be installed and used in RStudio, but there is no specific mention of version 4.5.1 being available or supported [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the availability of R version 4.5.1 in the module list.
2. If available, provide instructions to load the specific version of R.
3. If not available, escalate to the technical team to assess the feasibility of installing R version 4.5.1.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if R version 4.5.1 is listed in the module list?
- Are there any specific packages or dependencies required for your work that are only compatible with R version 4.5.1?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Heather,

Thank you for your request. We will need to verify the availability of R version 4.5.1 in our module list. If it is available, we will provide you with instructions on how to load it. If not, we will escalate your request to our technical team to assess the feasibility of installing this version.

Please confirm if R version 4.5.1 is listed in the module list. Additionally, let us know if there are any specific packages or dependencies required for your work that are only compatible with R version 4.5.1.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any new software versions are compatible with the existing system and do not introduce conflicts.
- Follow the standard procedure for software installation and updates.

rstudio to launch it.
Thanks,
Maciej

user_input:
Hi,
I am trying to run multi-xpu fine-tuning on the DAWN intel XPUs. I almost succeeded but there is an error raised.
According to Google, I have to install `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` to fix it.
[https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9](https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9)
The problem is that I do not have the sudo authentication.
Could you please help me install this package?
Thank you 
Best wishes,
Jiangnan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Package Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user needs to install the `level-zero-dev` package to resolve an error in multi-XPU fine-tuning on DAWN Intel XPUs. However, the user does not have sudo authentication to install the package themselves. Previous tickets indicate that package installation requests on DAWN are handled by the support team [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate the request to the appropriate team for package installation on DAWN.
2. Inform the user that the request has been escalated and provide an estimated timeline if possible.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Jiangnan,

Thank you for your request. The installation of the `level-zero-dev` package requires administrative privileges, which we will handle for you. We have escalated your request to the appropriate team for installation on DAWN.

We will keep you updated on the progress and provide an estimated timeline as soon as possible.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that all package installation requests are properly documented and escalated to the appropriate team [2].
- Do not provide sudo access to users for package installations.

REFERENCE KEY

[TICKET_SUMMARY] JAX on DAWN

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0010 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Good morning,
I’ve managed to get a large proportion of our code codes ported to GPU using JAX, an appropriate framework for our needs, which shows excellent performance at large scale.
It would be fantastic if these calculations could be run on DAWN. However, as documented in this thread, I have not had any success in installing it.
I understand there is no reason why it should not work on Intel machines, see the stable diffusion example below which is run on a similar hardware configuration as DAWN.
[https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)
It would be great if I could have some support in doing this, as I think our group and other potential JAX/DAWN users will benefit greatly from this.
Many thanks,
Lewis

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Torch dependencies installation in Dawn

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Simon,
Thank you for your email! Yes I saw that XPU support was not available for the additional packages, I tried to install the CPU versions to see if I could use them instead (and those processes would have to run on the CPU instead of the XPU, they're not directly involved in the training of the model but in the generation of the datasets ). I ran
pip install torch_scatter torch_sparse torch_cluster torch_spline_conv f [https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch](https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch)${TORCH_VERSION}+cpu.html<[https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch-$%7BTORCH_VERSION%7D+cpu.html](https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch-$%7BTORCH_VERSION%7D+cpu.html)>
Where the torch version was 2.3.1. The installation seemed to run correctly (I can see the packages listed when I run pip list), but the code doesnt run and I get the error I mentioned in my first email. These are the versions that I installed:
gpytorch                    1.14.2
intel_extension_for_pytorch 2.3.110+xpu
pytorch-ranger              0.1.1
torch                       2.3.1+cxx11.abi
torch_cluster               1.6.3+pt23cpu
torch-geometric             2.7.0
torch-optimizer             0.3.0
torch_scatter               2.1.2+pt23cpu
torch_sparse                0.6.18+pt23cpu
torch_spline_conv           1.2.2+pt23cpu
torchlens                   0.1.36
torchvision                 0.18.1+cxx11.abi.
Let me know if this is what you meant or you need more information. Thank you for the help!
Best,
Clara

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Clara,
Did you try creating a CPU Environment?
```
pip3 install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
pip install torch_geometric

# Optional dependencies:
pip install pyg_lib torch_scatter torch_sparse torch_cluster torch_spline_conv -f https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch-2.4.0+cpu.html
```
This maybe slower but might help find which parts are not optimized.
 Another Idea which I was looking at was investigation of the AMD ROCm version and see if it could be adapted to th intel XPU’s

SimonH

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Simon,
Unfortunately, the data we use to train the models is quite big, so we would need to run it on the GPU (CPU will be too slow). Do you mean setting up the CPU environment just to test what is not running correctly, or to swap to a CPU environment?
Thank you!
Clara

</MESSAGE>

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Simon,
Thank you for your email! Yes I saw that XPU support was not available for the additional packages, I tried to install the CPU versions to see if I could use them instead (and those processes would have to run on the CPU instead of the XPU, they're not directly involved in the training of the model but in the generation of the datasets ). I ran
pip install torch_scatter torch_sparse torch_cluster torch_spline_conv f [https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch](https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch)${TORCH_VERSION}+cpu.html<[https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch-$%7BTORCH_VERSION%7D+cpu.html](https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch-$%7BTORCH_VERSION%7D+cpu.html)>
Where the torch version was 2.3.1. The installation seemed to run correctly (I can see the packages listed when I run pip list), but the code doesnt run and I get the error I mentioned in my first email. These are the versions that I installed:
gpytorch                    1.14.2
intel_extension_for_pytorch 2.3.110+xpu
pytorch-ranger              0.1.1
torch                       2.3.1+cxx11.abi
torch_cluster               1.6.3+pt23cpu
torch-geometric             2.7.0
torch-optimizer             0.3.0
torch_scatter               2.1.2+pt23cpu
torch_sparse                0.6.18+pt23cpu
torch_spline_conv           1.2.2+pt23cpu
torchlens                   0.1.36
torchvision                 0.18.1+cxx11.abi.
Let me know if this is what you meant or you need more information. Thank you for the help!
Best,
Clara

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Clara,
Did you try creating a CPU Environment?
```
pip3 install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
pip install torch_geometric

# Optional dependencies:
pip install pyg_lib torch_scatter torch_sparse torch_cluster torch_spline_conv -f https://data.pyg.org/whl/torch-2.4.0+cpu.html
```
This maybe slower but might help find which parts are not optimized.
 Another Idea which I was looking at was investigation of the AMD ROCm version and see if it could be adapted to th intel XPU’s

SimonH

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Simon,
Unfortunately, the data we use to train the models is quite big, so we would need to run it on the GPU (CPU will be too slow). Do you mean setting up the CPU environment just to test what is not running correctly, or to swap to a CPU environment?
Thank you!
Clara

</MESSAGE>

user_input:
Hello HPC support team,

Name: Liam Collins-Jones, Account: lc984, PI: Gemma Bale
I would like to install Toast++ software (http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/research/vis/toast/download_src.html ). I attempted doing this myself towards the start of 2025 but was unsuccessful. Would anyone on your end be able to install this on my account? Happy to provide more details etc.
Thanks! Liam.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Source Build / User-Space Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation Request
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has requested the installation of Toast++ software on their account. Previous tickets show that software installation requests are handled by the HPC support team, and users are sometimes encouraged to attempt installation themselves [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Review the installation instructions provided by the user: http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/research/vis/toast/download_src.html.
2. Attempt to install Toast++ software on the user's account.
3. If installation is successful, inform the user.
4. If installation fails, escalate the issue to a higher support tier for further assistance.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Please provide any specific installation instructions or dependencies that might be required for Toast++.
- Have you attempted any specific steps for installation that failed? If so, please provide details.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Liam,

Thank you for your request. We will review the installation instructions for Toast++ software and attempt to install it on your account.

Please provide any specific installation instructions or dependencies that might be required for Toast++. Additionally, if you have attempted any specific steps for installation that failed, please provide details.

We will get back to you as soon as possible with an update.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any software installed complies with licensing and security policies.
- If the user has already attempted installation, gather details to avoid duplicating efforts [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[3] : HPCSSUP-80992::chunk::0001

user_input:
Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Module Request
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has requested an update to the latest version of BLAST, noting that the current version (2.11) is incomplete (e.g., missing blastp). Previous tickets indicate that different versions of BLAST have had issues, such as missing binaries and compatibility problems [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate the request to the HPC support team for further investigation and potential module update.
2. Verify the availability and completeness of the latest BLAST version.
3. Ensure that the new module includes all necessary binaries (e.g., blastp).

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your request. We have escalated your request to our HPC support team to investigate the possibility of updating the BLAST module to the latest version. We will ensure that the new module includes all necessary binaries, such as blastp.

We will keep you updated on the progress.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any new module is thoroughly tested before making it available to users.
- Communicate any updates or changes to the user promptly.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-76860::chunk::0001
[2] : HPCSSUP-32047::chunk::0002

[TICKET_SUMMARY] remote blast against nt database fails

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0002b role=TICKET_CREATOR part=2/2 original_id=0002>
---
 
Von: Leon Bond <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Gesendet: Mittwoch, 3. Februar 2021 17:43
An: M. Nackenhorst <mn480@cam.ac.uk>
Betreff: HPCSSUP-32047 remote blast against nt database fails 
   
—-—-—-—
Reply above this line.
Leon Bond commented:
Dear Maja,
I believe that the version of blast we have on the cluster is too old (v2.4) to make secure connections to the remote site, i.e. https:// instead of http://
The blast documentation sugggests that v2.5+ is needed for this ([1][https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/))
We've built a v2.9 module of blast, which you can try by running
module load /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-skylake_avx512/blast-plus-2.9.0-gcc-9.3.0-h4vn5dr 
That isn't currently visible to users by default, as we've had to use a different version of the 'spack' software packaging tool for this. In the near future we'll be making changes that will allow this to be visible via a search.
Could you try using this module and see if it works for you instead of blast/2.4.0 ?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

[TICKET_SUMMARY] remote blast against nt database fails

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Maja,
I believe that the version of blast we have on the cluster is too old (v2.4) to make secure connections to the remote site, i.e. https:// instead of http://
The blast documentation sugggests that v2.5+ is needed for this ([https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/))
We've built a v2.9 module of blast, which you can try by running
```java
module load /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-skylake_avx512/blast-plus-2.9.0-gcc-9.3.0-h4vn5dr
```
That isn't currently visible to users by default, as we've had to use a different version of the 'spack' software packaging tool for this. In the near future we'll be making changes that will allow this to be visible via a search.
Could you try using this module and see if it works for you instead of blast/2.4.0 ?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0002a role=TICKET_CREATOR part=1/2 original_id=0002>
Dear Leon,  
thank you for your quick response! I send the job off again with the new module and will report back hopefully tomorrow.  
regards,  
Maja

</MESSAGE>

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment / Conda Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue described is a common problem with activating Conda environments in SLURM batch jobs. The environment activates correctly on the login node but not in the SLURM job. This is likely due to the environment not being properly initialized in the SLURM job script [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the SLURM job script for proper initialization of the Conda environment.
2. Ensure that the `conda init` command is included in the script to initialize Conda.
3. Check that the `source .bashrc` command is used to reload the shell configuration after initializing Conda.
4. Confirm that the correct Conda environment is activated using `conda activate ml`.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Stefanos,

Thank you for your detailed query. The issue you're experiencing with Conda environment activation in SLURM jobs is a common one. Please ensure that your SLURM job script includes the following steps:

1. Initialize Conda:
```bash
conda init --all
```

2. Reload the shell configuration:
```bash
source ~/.bashrc
```

3. Activate the Conda environment:
```bash
conda activate ml
```

Your updated SLURM job script should look like this:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake

# Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
module load miniconda

# Initialize Conda
conda init --all

# Reload the shell configuration
source ~/.bashrc

# Activate the environment
conda activate ml

# Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"

# Alternatively:
echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"

# Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
```

Please try this updated script and let us know if it resolves the issue.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the Conda environment is properly initialized and activated in the SLURM job script to avoid environment-related errors [1][2][3][4][5].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-16898::chunk::0000
[2] : HPCSSUP-52206::chunk::0001
[3] : HPCSSUP-65220::chunk::0000
[4] : HPCSSUP-26914::chunk::0000
[5] : HPCSSUP-83117::chunk::0000

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
 (5 kB)

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Akshay,
The order of the commands to activate your environment should be:
```java
#! Optionally modify the environment seen by the application
#! (note that SLURM reproduces the environment at submission irrespective of ~/.bashrc):
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh                # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge                               # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-icl              # REQUIRED - loads the basic environment#! Insert additional module load commands after this line if needed: 
module load python/3.8 
module load miniconda/3

conda init --all 
source /home/aj686/.bashrc
conda activate euclid
```
Please change your submission script and try again. Let me know if this worked for you.
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0003a role=TICKET_CREATOR part=1/2 original_id=0003>
Dear Avraam,

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Activating conda environment 

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support Team,
Hope this email finds you well.
I am writing to ask for help when activating a conda environment inside a SLURM job. I have submitted multiple jobs with the following script (activating the conda environment using `source activate`):
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J castep-xyz
#SBATCH -A MOYA-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=28
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH -p icelake
#! Output filename:
#! %A means slurm job ID and %a means array index
#SBATCH --output=castep_alpha_1_%A.out
#! Errors filename:
#SBATCH --error=castep_alpha_1_%A.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#! ###############################
#! Script to run CASTEP from ASE
#! ###############################
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-icl # Loads the basic environment
module load castep/impi/22.11 # Load CASTEP
module load miniconda/3 # Load miniconda, required otherwise cannot activate conda env
source activate /home/jiq21/.conda/envs/conv
export I_MPI_PIN_DOMAIN=omp:compact
export I_MPI_PIN_ORDER=scatter
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e 's/^([0-9][0-9]).$/\1/')
#! Work directory (i.e. where the job will run):
workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR" # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 32:
#export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
ARRAYID=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID, ArrayID: $ARRAYID \n======"
echo "Time: `date`"
echo "Running on master node: `hostname`"
echo "Current directory: `pwd`"
echo "\nnumtasks=$numtasks, numnodes=$numnodes, mpi_tasks_per_node=$mpi_tasks_per_node (OMP_NUM_THREADS=$OMP_NUM_THREADS)"
command="mpirun -ppn $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np castep.mpi"
pp_path="../../CASTEP_pspots/"
output_path="../../database-dft/"
traj_files=(*.lammpstrj) # get all lammpstrj files in the database
traj_file=${traj_files[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID-1]}
output_file=${output_path}/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_dft_1.xyz # remove .xyz from the file name
1. alpha: 10 frames of equilibration, skip every other 2 frames (33)
2. --index="10:90:2" (40)
3. using pp_path, dir_path and task as default
python /home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py \
--command="${command}" \
--dirpath="../../CASTEP_calcs/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_1/calc/" \
--pppath="${pp_path}" \
--filename="${traj_file}" \
--output="${output_file}" \
--task="singlepoint" \
--xcfunc="PBEsol" \
--cutoffenergy=750 \
--kp=0.028 \
--electol=1e-8 \
--index="11:77:2"
They were running without errors until yesterday, when I had the error:
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py", line 6, in <module>
from dft_calc import *
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/dft_calc.py", line 5, in <module>
from ase import units, Atoms
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ase'
For the job with ID 5113464, which was an array job queued for 10 days. I have resent the job using: `sbatch —array=1 —time=00:01:00 -N1 -n1 sub_ase_castep_alpha_1` (script in the path: /home/jiq21/rds/hpc-work/year_3/AgI_mace_train_try2/md-database/1kbar/ , charged to account MOYA-SL3-CPU) and it ran without an environment problem (although obviously timed out before computing anything).
My worry is that I have multiple jobs with a similar script in the queue since the 12th and I am not sure whether they will run successfully or have a problem activating the conda environment. Would you be able to give any suggestions on why the previous job failed? I did not send it while the environment was activated in the login node. I had tried that previously and didn’t succeed on activating the environment.
Kind regards,
Javiera Quiroz
PhD student
Department of Material Science
University of Cambridge

workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR" # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 32:
#export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
ARRAYID=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID, ArrayID: $ARRAYID \n======"
echo "Time: `date`"
echo "Running on master node: `hostname`"
echo "Current directory: `pwd`"
echo "\nnumtasks=$numtasks, numnodes=$numnodes, mpi_tasks_per_node=$mpi_tasks_per_node (OMP_NUM_THREADS=$OMP_NUM_THREADS)"
command="mpirun -ppn $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np castep.mpi"
pp_path="../../CASTEP_pspots/"
output_path="../../database-dft/"
traj_files=(*.lammpstrj) # get all lammpstrj files in the database
traj_file=${traj_files[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID-1]}
output_file=${output_path}/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_dft_1.xyz # remove .xyz from the file name
1. alpha: 10 frames of equilibration, skip every other 2 frames (33)
2. --index="10:90:2" (40)
3. using pp_path, dir_path and task as default
python /home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py \
--command="${command}" \
--dirpath="../../CASTEP_calcs/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_1/calc/" \
--pppath="${pp_path}" \
--filename="${traj_file}" \
--output="${output_file}" \
--task="singlepoint" \
--xcfunc="PBEsol" \
--cutoffenergy=750 \
--kp=0.028 \
--electol=1e-8 \
--index="11:77:2"
They were running without errors until yesterday, when I had the error:
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py", line 6, in <module>
from dft_calc import *
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/dft_calc.py", line 5, in <module>
from ase import units, Atoms
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ase'
For the job with ID 5113464, which was an array job queued for 10 days. I have resent the job using: `sbatch —array=1 —time=00:01:00 -N1 -n1 sub_ase_castep_alpha_1` (script in the path: /home/jiq21/rds/hpc-work/year_3/AgI_mace_train_try2/md-database/1kbar/ , charged to account MOYA-SL3-CPU) and it ran without an environment problem (although obviously timed out before computing anything).
My worry is that I have multiple jobs with a similar script in the queue since the 12th and I am not sure whether they will run successfully or have a problem activating the conda environment. Would you be able to give any suggestions on why the previous job failed? I did not send it while the environment was activated in the login node. I had tried that previously and didn’t succeed on activating the environment.
Kind regards,
Javiera Quiroz
PhD student
Department of Material Science
University of Cambridge

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Slow Conda environment setup and activation on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
I am a PhD student in the Engineering Department, division F, have been experiencing some significant delays with conda environment setup and activation on CSD3, and am attaching details below which I hope can be helpful in diagnosing the issue.
1. Environment build failures
I had to make three attempts to build a conda environment with PyTorch 2.8.0 and pyannote.audio 4.0.3. The first two jobs timed out despite using very little CPU or memory. The bottleneck appears to be network I/O when downloading packages. Here are the details:
Job ID
State
Wall-clock
CPU Used
Memory Used
21592679
TIMEOUT (killed during "Verifying transaction")
02:00:11
00:01:22 (0.28%)
357 MB (2.18%)
21596158
TIMEOUT (killed during "Installing pip dependencies")
04:00:08
00:02:00 (0.21%)
508 MB (3.10%)
21629759
COMPLETED
00:07:36
00:01:17 (3.38%)
278 MB (1.70%)
The third job completed quickly because the packages were already cached from the previous two attempts.
2. Conda activation and script runtime speed
Since last Thursday, activating conda environment has been notably slow. I had to remove the conda initialisation lines from my ~/.bashrc file in order to access the login node. I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Cannot set-up conda environment on CSD3 HPC

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear CSD3 team,
I have encountered a hurdle when trying to set-up and run computations on your cluster for the first time.
I am unable to successfully set-up my Conda environment with the packages that I require to run my Python script. I have been working to resolve this issue for multiple days now but at no avail.
Initially, I started by trying to re-create the Conda environment that I currently work on within a different  compute cluster. When this did not work, I simply tried installing one package at a time, but would still encounter the same issue regardless of how simple I tried to make the process.
The error I keep encountering (many times, sometimes in slightly differing error messages) is that the environment is inconsistent and it fails to resolve. Here are the packages that I need to install to allow my code to run:
bctpy – Brain Connectivity Toolbox (Python version; installable via pip)
*
h5py
*
matplotlib
*
networkx
*
numpy
*
pandas
*
plotly
*
scikit-learn
*
scipy
*
seaborn
*
torch
*
umap-learn
I have tried different solutions including updating conda (was not able to do this, same issue) and installing mamba (was not able to do this, same issue). I am not very knowledgeable about these things - but I have been able to work with conda successfully on another compute cluster, no issue.
I would very much appreciate any help that you could give me. I am available at any time to provide any more information, or to work through this through videocall/screenshare if this is something that you do.
Thanks and best wishes,
El

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Conda not usable, very slow Python execution

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC team,
I'm trying to activate my Conda environment for course work (`MLMI2`)
and the conda environment simply doesn't load (`source /rds/project/rds-xyBFuSj0hm0/MLMI2.M2025/miniconda3/bin/activate`).
As a workaround, I've set the locale manually in the environment. Once that is done, it works fine.
Trying to run any python files afterwards takes ages (waiting > 3 mins with no result for a script that used to run < 10s).
Also: connecting to my github account and fetching updates I've written on my local machine takes significantly longer than before.
Do you have an idea what this might be caused by? This has started today, so it might be connected with the throttling ([https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/data_centre_upgrade.html#key-dates](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/data_centre_upgrade.html#key-dates))?
Yesterday everything worked fine.
This affects all multiple machines I've provisioned (in interactive sessions).
Thank you in advance for your support!
- Konstantin Sturtzkopf

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Extremely Slow Executions

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Sir or Madam,
I am still experiencing extremely slow runtime, when loading python libraries, to the extend, that it is impossible to work. I have been talking to several people, and the problem, which has persisted since yesterday, is also present for them.
I am logged in under lv380
For reproducibility on cpu-q-478 (but also present on gpu and login nodes)
>source /home/lv380/rds/hpc-work/thesis/MD/mace-md/.uv_env/bin/activate
>python -v /home/lv380/rds/hpc-work/thesis/MD/mace-md/mace_md/cli/entrypoint.py
Already running
>source /home/lv380/rds/hpc-work/thesis/MD/mace-md/.uv_env/bin/activate
>python
>import numpy
Does sometimes take several minutes.
I have been running the exact same commands around 2-3am today. Back then it worked better, which is why I assume it to be related to overall load.
I hope it is possible to resolve this.
Kind regards,
Leonhard Vulpius

user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Dear support team,
I am trying to log in to my RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla. I am following the instructions given in "Reseach Storage Documentation”. Unfortunately, I am denied access after entering my password and the TOTP verification code (see screenshot).
I can succesfully log in to [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/), so I assume I have the correct password. I have also succesfully set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 (see screenshot), and I am correctly entering the code when prompted to do so.
Maybe I need a different password that is specific for this service?
Kind regards
Christina
Prof. Dr. med. Christina Zielinski
Professor of Infection and Immunity
Department of Pathology
University of Cambridge
Tennis Court Road
Cambridge CB2 1QP
cez22@cam.ac.uk
s[Screenshot 2026-03-08 at 09.42.33.png]
[IMG_5398.PNG]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing access issues with RCS storage using FileZilla, despite successfully logging into other HPC services and setting up MFA/TOTP. Similar issues have been reported by other users, suggesting a common problem with TOTP configuration or authentication [1][2][3][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's access permissions to the RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski).
2. Check if the user has the correct TOTP code labeled as "CSD3:SSH" in their authenticator app.
3. If the TOTP code is incorrect or missing, reset the MFA for the user.
4. Provide the user with the correct QR code to scan for setting up TOTP.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you are using the TOTP code labeled as "CSD3:SSH" in your authenticator app?
- Have you tried accessing the RCS storage from different networks (e.g., work, home, with/without VPN)?
- Have you tried using a different authenticator app or device to scan the QR code?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Christina,

Thank you for your message. It appears that there might be an issue with the TOTP configuration for your RCS storage access. We will verify your access permissions and check the TOTP code in your authenticator app.

If necessary, we may need to reset your MFA and provide you with a new QR code to scan. Please confirm that you are using the TOTP code labeled as "CSD3:SSH" in your authenticator app.

We will keep you updated on the progress.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user has the correct access permissions before proceeding with any troubleshooting steps.
- Do not share sensitive information such as passwords or TOTP codes over email or chat.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-72734::chunk::0000
[2] : HPCSSUP-51042::chunk::0000
[3] : HPCSSUP-60259::chunk::0003
[4] : HPCSSUP-80997::chunk::0001

user_input:
Good evening,
I have recently been trying to connect to the CSD3 to start working in the cluster for the first time. I have Two-Factor Authentication on my account already enabled (please see image attached).
When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
Many thanks for your assistance on this matter.
Martin
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 January 2026 14:44
To: Martin Santamarina Garcia <ms3242@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (ms3242)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / MFA Reset
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is unable to generate a new TOTP verification code because they did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time. This issue has been reported by other users [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's identity and account details.
2. Reset the TOTP MFA secret for the user's account.
3. Provide the user with a new QR code to scan with their authenticator app.
4. Ensure the user can successfully log in with the new TOTP verification code.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm your CRSid and the email address associated with your account?
- Have you tried logging in with your Raven password and the TOTP verification code from your authenticator app?
- Have you received any error messages during the login process?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

Thank you for your message. We can reset the TOTP MFA secret for your account and provide you with a new QR code to scan with your authenticator app. Please confirm your CRSid and the email address associated with your account.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always verify the user's identity before resetting their MFA secret.
- Ensure the user can successfully log in with the new TOTP verification code before closing the ticket.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-52352::chunk::0000
[2] : HPCSSUP-60021::chunk::0000
[3] : HPCSSUP-101511::chunk::0000
[4] : HPCSSUP-79973::chunk::0000
[5] : HPCSSUP-68955::chunk::0000

see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.)](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.))
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU 
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
 
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
 
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
 
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
 
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
 
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Ice Lake and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S

docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI

SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
